title: MNF-248 contactsStore lifecycle cleanup across user selectors
category: Frontend Architecture / State Management / Workflow UX Stability

ownership_classification.background: PM/product direction and issue scope defined the problem boundaries: a shared global contactsStore caused cross-UI state interference across Workspace List and in-workspace selectors.
ownership_classification.t1_contribution: Analyzed the technical scope and root cause as a shared-store lifecycle problem, clarified impacted components and flows, and aligned the low-risk solution direction: keep the singleton store, add reliable cleanup, and preserve visible selection state in the Workspace List filter.
ownership_classification.t2_contribution: Implemented and refined the web solution in affected Vue components by ensuring destroyStore() runs on selector unmount/close and by updating ContactListFilterSelect.vue to cache and display the selected label even after the shared store resets.
ownership_classification.coordination: Worked within a cross-flow implementation context spanning Workspace List filtering, Automation Send Email, DocuSign signer selection, invite coordinator, and reassignment flows, with attention to regression prevention and behavior consistency.
ownership_classification.outcome: Reduced cross-UI interference from shared contact state, stabilized user-visible selection behavior, and delivered the fix with minimal refactor risk by preserving the existing singleton-store architecture.

summary: MNF-248 was a frontend stability and state-management improvement in Moxo New Flow focused on fixing cross-UI interference caused by a shared global Pinia contactsStore used by multiple contact-selection experiences. The issue affected the Workspace List contact filter as well as in-workspace selectors such as Automation Send Email, DocuSign signer selection, coordinator invitation, and member/action reassignment. My contribution centered on technical analysis and structured web implementation: I helped define the low-risk solution approach of keeping the singleton store, enforcing lifecycle cleanup when selectors unmount, and preserving the Workspace List’s selected contact label through local caching so the UI remains stable after store reset.
ownership_description: I did not make the product decision to redesign the architecture; the chosen direction was to avoid a larger refactor and keep a single global contactsStore. My ownership was on the technical side: analyzing why cross-UI interference happened, identifying the affected selectors and user flows, translating the solution into component-level web behavior, and implementing the frontend changes in Vue. Specifically, I was responsible for lifecycle cleanup in the shared selector flows and for the ContactListFilterSelect.vue behavior that preserved the selected label independently from the volatile shared contact list.
technical_highlights: Analyzed a shared-state interference issue where multiple UIs mutated the same Pinia contactsStore state, including contactList, filterParams, pagination state, and loading flags.
Chose a low-risk fix strategy that preserved the singleton-store architecture instead of introducing scoped or multi-instance stores.
Implemented destroyStore() cleanup on unmount/close in shared selector components such as MxUserSelector.vue and MxDDRAndUserEmailSelector.vue.
Updated ContactListFilterSelect.vue to cache the selected label locally so the Workspace List UI still shows the chosen contact after shared store reset.
Separated user-visible selection state from volatile list data by adding cachedSelectedLabel and fallback selectedOptionLabel logic.
Improved workflow consistency across multiple business flows that reused the same contact-selection mechanism.
resume_bullets: Resolved cross-UI state interference in a shared Vue/Pinia contactsStore used by multiple contact-selection workflows in Moxo New Flow.
Implemented lifecycle cleanup with destroyStore() in shared selector components to prevent stale contact state from leaking across Workspace List and in-workspace flows.
Redesigned ContactListFilterSelect.vue to preserve selected-label display through local caching, keeping UI behavior stable after shared store reset.
Supported regression-safe frontend delivery across Workspace List filtering, Automation Send Email, DocuSign signer selection, coordinator invitation, and member reassignment flows.
Delivered a low-risk state-management fix by strengthening store lifecycle handling without requiring a disruptive architectural refactor.
interview_answer: One project that reflects my frontend systems thinking is MNF-248, which focused on shared-state interference across user selectors in Moxo New Flow. Several contact-selection experiences, such as the Workspace List filter, automation email recipient selector, and general user selectors inside a workspace, all reused the same global Pinia contactsStore. Because they shared mutable state like contactList, filters, pagination, and loading flags, actions in one UI could overwrite another and create confusing behavior. My role was not to redefine the product direction, but to analyze the technical scope and implement a safe web solution. The agreed approach was to avoid a risky architecture rewrite and keep the singleton store. I implemented lifecycle cleanup so destroyStore() runs when the relevant selector components unmount, and I updated ContactListFilterSelect.vue so the selected contact label is cached locally and still shown even if the shared store has been reset by another selector. That project is a good example of how I handle complex shared-state problems: understand the lifecycle, separate volatile data from stable user-visible state, and deliver a low-risk fix across interconnected workflows.
self_intro: I’m a frontend engineer with strong experience in Vue-based workflow products, especially where shared state, reusable components, and multi-flow behavior need careful design. For example, in MNF-248 I worked on a shared contactsStore lifecycle issue across several contact-selection flows, combining technical analysis with structured web implementation to prevent cross-UI interference while keeping the existing architecture stable.
talking_points: This was fundamentally a shared-store lifecycle problem, not just a dropdown bug.
The difficulty came from multiple independent UIs reusing and mutating one global contactsStore.
I focused on technical analysis, impacted-flow identification, and translating the solution into safe frontend behavior.
The solution intentionally avoided a large architectural refactor and instead improved lifecycle cleanup plus UI-state preservation.
A key implementation detail was separating stable user-visible selection state from volatile shared list data.
The ContactListFilterSelect fix used cachedSelectedLabel and fallback label logic so the UI remained consistent after store reset.
The work required regression awareness across Workspace List, automation, DocuSign, invite coordinator, and reassignment flows.
This project is a strong example of my ability to work on complex web application behavior with structured, low-risk implementation.

context.business_background: The issue originated from a bug where the Workspace List should keep the previously selected contact after opening coordinator view and returning to the list. The root cause was broader than that single symptom: several contact-selection experiences reused one global contactsStore, causing state interference across workflows.
context.team_client_requirements: The required behavior was to prevent shared-store contamination across selectors while keeping existing user flows reliable. The Workspace List contact filter needed to continue showing the selected label even if the contact list was cleared due to store reset elsewhere.
context.pm_decisions: The chosen solution direction was to keep a single global store rather than introduce scoped or multi-instance store architecture, in order to minimize refactor risk. The fix focused on reliable destroyStore() cleanup and UI-state preservation in the Workspace List filter.
context.t1_responsibilities: Technical scope analysis included understanding the shared-store lifecycle issue, identifying the main conflict points between Workspace List filtering and in-workspace selectors, clarifying the safe solution boundary, and defining the expected behavior across affected flows and components.
context.t2_responsibilities: Web implementation work included ensuring destroyStore() is called in selector/search components on unmount or close, especially MxUserSelector.vue and MxDDRAndUserEmailSelector.vue, and updating ContactListFilterSelect.vue with cached selected-label behavior and fallback display logic.
context.architecture_details: At the architecture level, the problem sat in the store/view interaction layer. A singleton Pinia contactsStore held shared state such as contactList, filterParams, pagination cursor/sequence, hasMore, and loading flags. Multiple selector views and filter components read and mutated the same store. The fix preserved the store design but improved lifecycle cleanup and added component-local UI state caching to decouple visible selection from shared list volatility.
context.coordination: The work affected and needed alignment across several flows: Workspace List contact filter, Automation Send Email user selection, DocuSign signer selection, invite coordinator, and member/action reassignment. Coordination emphasis was on keeping behavior consistent and preventing regressions rather than changing business logic.
context.challenges: The main challenge was solving cross-UI interference in a reusable shared-state mechanism without causing broad regression through architectural refactor. Another challenge was ensuring the Workspace List still displayed the correct selected contact even after the shared store had been reset by another selector.
context.outcomes: The final result improved frontend consistency across shared contact-selection flows, prevented stale or overwritten contact state from leaking between UIs, preserved the selected label display in the Workspace List, and delivered the fix through a low-risk implementation strategy.

raw_text: MNF-248 addressed contactsStore lifecycle cleanup across user selectors in Moxo New Flow. The issue came from several contact-selection UIs sharing one global Pinia contactsStore, which led to state interference across Workspace List filtering and in-workspace selectors. My work focused on technical scope analysis and web implementation: clarifying the shared-store lifecycle problem, identifying the affected flows, implementing destroyStore() cleanup on selector unmount, and updating ContactListFilterSelect.vue to cache the selected label so the UI remains stable after store reset. The project demonstrates my strengths in structured frontend problem solving, state-management analysis, cross-flow consistency, and low-risk implementation in a complex workflow application.

title: Untitled Project

ownership_classification.background: Flow functionality was evolving to support more advanced scenarios such as DDR and Trigger, which exposed weaknesses in the existing role model. The product needed more flexible role behavior in templates and runtime workspaces so users could preserve role definitions, defer assignment where appropriate, and reuse common assignments more efficiently. :contentReference[oaicite:30]{index=30} The project required consistent role behavior across Web, iOS, Android, BIZ Server, REST API, and iSDK. It also needed to support real workflow usage patterns, including optional role assignment at creation time, runtime reassignment, and action-specific compatibility requirements. :contentReference[oaicite:31]{index=31} PM-defined scope included four major decisions: retain unassigned roles in flow template creation/editing, allow template roles to remain unassigned when creating flows from templates, tune step-start logic when roles appear in assignees, and support a default assignee for template roles. PM also defined important exceptions, such as keeping some role usages mandatory for restricted action types and preserving stricter validation for scheduled flow workspace scenarios. :contentReference[oaicite:32]{index=32}
ownership_classification.t1_contribution: My T1 work included technical scope analysis, skill/platform assessment, implementation estimation, and defining behavior/interface expectations across platforms and layers. I translated business scenarios into technical rules, exception handling, and engineering-ready definitions. :contentReference[oaicite:33]{index=33}
ownership_classification.t2_contribution: My T2 work focused on web development and detailed implementation design, including role data type updates, workflow role conversion, controller/template default-user loading, store validation, participant form behavior, selector updates, and UI rendering for default assignees and unassigned roles. :contentReference[oaicite:34]{index=34}
ownership_classification.coordination: The work required alignment across web, mobile, BIZ Server, REST API, and iSDK, including shared interpretation of runtime role behavior, API-level behavior, and special-case action support. This included coordinated handling for DocuSign, Time Booking, Jumio, and role-based automation actions. :contentReference[oaicite:36]{index=36}
ownership_classification.outcome: The project produced a more flexible and practical workflow role experience: roles could remain unassigned in templates, template-based creation no longer forced premature assignment in allowed cases, default assignees improved efficiency for repeated role patterns, and role behavior became more aligned with real runtime workflow needs.

raw_text: MV-17305 Flow Role Usage Experience Tuning improved how roles behave in flow templates and runtime workspaces. The scope included retaining unassigned roles, allowing optional assignment when creating flows from templates, tuning runtime step-start behavior when roles are unresolved, and supporting default assignees for template roles. My role was to convert PM-defined requirements into technical behavior across platforms in T1 and to own the web-side detailed design and implementation in T2. On the web side, I worked through data/entity/model, controller, store, and view layers to ensure correct role modeling, participant handling, validation changes, selector behavior, and default-user support, while coordinating edge-case handling across client and server layers.

title: Workflow File Variable & Update Workspace Info Automation

ownership_classification.background: This work was part of a larger New Flow task group spanning MV-17980, MNF-30, MNF-33, and MNF-548. The broader scope included workspace variable expansion and new automation capabilities, while my focus was limited to workflow file variables and the Update Workspace Info / Update Template-Flow Info automation path.
ownership_classification.t1_contribution: In T1, I was responsible for technical scope analysis, web/platform assessment, implementation breakdown, and behavior/data/interface definition for file-type workspace variables and their use inside Update Workspace Info automation. This included clarifying payload shape, attachment-variable mapping, runtime/display behavior, and coordination boundaries between web, integration server, and application server.
ownership_classification.t2_contribution: In T2, I handled the web-side detailed design and implementation for file variable flows, including file uploader behavior, variable CRUD/rendering, variable display ordering, automation add/edit/submit/load flows, and refactoring file-variable logic into dedicated utility modules for maintainability.
ownership_classification.coordination: I coordinated closely with PM/T1/T2 and server-side counterparts to ensure the web implementation matched backend processing for temp board uploads, resource copy, Variables folder behavior, automation attachment preservation, file variable persistence, and post-execution rendering. Product decisions remained with PM/P-owners; my role was to translate them into technically consistent web behavior and interface contracts.
ownership_classification.outcome: The delivered scope enabled file-type workspace variables to function through template configuration, start-flow/runtime editing, DDR/file source selection, and Update Workspace Info automation, with consistent UI behavior and server-aligned data handling. Related tasks and sub-tasks were marked completed/approved on the web side.

summary: Worked on a workflow platform initiative focused on file-type workspace variables and file-variable support in Update Workspace Info automation. My contribution centered on technical scope analysis, cross-platform behavior and data/interface definition, and web-side implementation across builder, runtime execution, preview, and post-execution states. I helped turn PM-defined product requirements into a maintainable web solution covering file upload flows, variable modeling, attachment-variable mapping, ordering, validation, preview logic, and coordination with integration/application server behavior.
ownership_description: I was responsible for the parts related to workflow file variables and the automation path for updating template/flow info. Specifically, I handled T1 technical analysis and interface definition for file-type workspace variables and their behavior inside Update Workspace Info automation, then carried the web-side T2 design and implementation for uploader behavior, payload structure, variable rendering, file-variable persistence, preview/runtime display, and refactoring into reusable utilities. I did not own the overall product direction or all automation types; those were broader task-group decisions defined by PM and shared across teams.
technical_highlights: Defined FILE as a supported workspace variable type on web, with supporting structures such as EWorkspaceVariableType.FILE and IWorkflowFileVariableInfo containing files and temp_board_id.

Designed and implemented file-variable handling across template configuration, flow execution, and runtime workspace editing using temp board uploads, variable-file sequence mapping, and persisted string_value conversion.

Enabled file-type workspace variables to be exposed as DDR sources with EDDRFieldType.FILE and workspace-variable subtype support, so file variables could participate in automation/file flows beyond manual upload.

Added file-variable support to Update Workspace Info automation through a dedicated workspace_file_variables model, separate from workspace_variables, with attachments mapped by customData.variableId.

Defined end-to-end add/edit/submit/load behavior for automation file variables, including upload, validation, save, and rehydration phases, so file attachments could be reconstructed back into variable-specific uploader state. The sequence diagrams on the design pages show this flow explicitly.

Handled mixed file sources for automation and variable editing, including static files, DDR files, and web links where supported, while distinguishing UX between start-link execution and coordinator runtime editing.
Implemented variableDisplayOrder to preserve combined ordering of normal variables and file variables in builder, preview, and runtime views.

Refactored file-variable logic into dedicated utility modules, centralizing file detection, attachment loading, payload construction, sync/copy/delete behavior, and cross-workspace file operations for better maintainability.

Aligned web behavior with server-side logic for copying files into the Variables folder and resolving final string_value resource paths after execution. The diagrams and T1/T2 docs show the copy-to-Variables-folder flow and the resulting resource-based string_value format.
resume_bullets: Led T1 technical scoping and web/platform interface definition for file-type workspace variables and their use in Update Workspace Info automation within a workflow platform.

Implemented web-side support for FILE workspace variables across template builder, flow execution, and runtime workspace editing, including temp-board upload flow, payload persistence, and DDR exposure.
Designed and delivered file-variable support in Update Workspace Info automation using separate workspace_file_variables modeling and attachment-to-variable mapping via customData.variableId.
Built and refined add/edit/submit/load flows for automation file variables, including validation, preview/runtime display, invalid DDR handling, and post-execution attachment reconstruction.

Refactored file-variable logic into reusable utility modules to centralize attachment loading, payload building, synchronization, and cross-board copy/delete behavior, improving maintainability of the Vue web implementation.

Coordinated web delivery with integration/application server logic for file copy, Variables folder handling, and persisted resource-path updates, ensuring consistent builder-to-execution behavior across the workflow system.
interview_answer: One representative project I worked on was a workflow platform feature set around file-type workspace variables and Update Workspace Info automation. The broader initiative covered several related tasks, but my main responsibility was the part where file variables had to work end to end inside the workflow system rather than behave like simple form uploads. In T1, I translated PM-defined requirements into technical scope, data structures, and cross-platform behavior, especially how file variables should be modeled, uploaded through temp boards, exposed to DDR, and mapped inside automation configuration. In T2, I implemented the web-side solution in a structured way across model, controller, store, and view layers. A key challenge was that file variables could not reuse the text/list variable path. They needed separate handling for attachments, variable ordering, preview/runtime rendering, and post-execution reconstruction. For Update Workspace Info automation, I helped define and implement workspace_file_variables plus attachment metadata through customData.variableId so files could be reliably associated with the correct variable. I also worked on uploader behavior differences between flow execution and runtime editing, and refactored file-variable logic into reusable utilities for loading, payload building, and synchronization. Overall, the project shows my strength in technical analysis, interface definition, structured Vue implementation, and coordinating complex stateful behavior across web and server-side systems.
self_intro: I’m a web developer with experience building complex workflow features where UI behavior needs to stay tightly aligned with backend contracts and system state. A good example is my work on file-type workspace variables and Update Workspace Info automation in a workflow platform, where I handled technical scoping, interface definition, and Vue-based implementation across builder, runtime, preview, and execution flows. My strengths are structured problem solving, cross-team coordination, and turning complicated product requirements into maintainable web architecture.
talking_points: My scope was focused: workflow file variables and Update Workspace Info / update template-flow info automation, not the entire task group.
I handled both T1 and T2 for my portion: technical analysis and interface definition first, then web-side detailed design and implementation.
The hard part was not uploading files; it was making file variables behave correctly across builder, execution, runtime editing, preview, and executed states.
File variables required separate storage and mapping from text/list variables, which is why workspace_file_variables and attachment customData.variableId were introduced.
I worked through the full web architecture path: data/entity/model to controller to store to view, rather than only building UI screens.
I helped refactor file-variable logic into dedicated utilities to improve maintainability and reduce feature-specific logic leakage into generic variable handling.

A major part of the work was aligning web behavior with integration/application server processing, especially temp board uploads, file copy, Variables folder creation, and final resource-path persistence.
This project is a strong example of my fit for Vue web roles, complex web application roles, workflow-system roles, and cross-functional execution roles. :contentReference[oaicite:71]{index=71}

context.business_background: The business goal was to expand workflow platform capability by supporting richer workspace variables and more powerful internal automations. Workspace variables were extended beyond text/list to include files, and Update Workspace Info automation needed to update variable values, including file variables, in a usable and consistent way.
context.team_client_requirements: The system needed to support one or more files per variable, file variable editing during flow execution and in runtime workspaces, DDR exposure, coordinator preview/download behavior, Variables folder visibility, and automation-driven updates of workspace/file variable values. For Update Workspace Info automation, supported sources included static files, DDR files, and later web links where required.
context.pm_decisions: PM/product decisions included introducing FILE as a new workspace variable type, adding Update Workspace Info as a new automation, simplifying start-link file upload UX to desktop-first, keeping richer source options for coordinator runtime editing, and ensuring automation-based file-variable updates append files rather than replace existing ones. Those decisions were product-owned; my role was to turn them into technically coherent web behavior and implementation.
context.t1_responsibilities: My T1 work included technical scope analysis, implementation estimation, platform/skill assessment, defining behavior and data/interface contracts, documenting automation CRUD flows, clarifying file-variable payload shape, specifying attachment-variable mapping, and aligning the web-side solution with server-side execution/update behavior. The CRUD and execution diagrams on the design pages illustrate this definition work.
context.t2_responsibilities: My T2 work focused on detailed web design and implementation: supporting FILE workspace variables in the UI, implementing file uploader flows, exposing file variables as DDR sources, supporting file variables in Update Workspace Info automation add/edit/submit/load flows, preserving variable order, handling preview/runtime display, and refactoring into dedicated file-variable utilities.
context.architecture_details: At the data/model level, the implementation introduced FILE variable typing, IWorkflowFileVariableInfo, workspace_file_variables, variableDisplayOrder, and attachment customData.variableId. At the controller/store level, logic handled loading pre-uploaded files, splitting workspace_variables vs workspace_file_variables, syncing files across boards, preserving metadata, and reconstructing file attachments after execution. At the view level, MultipleFilesUploader and workspace variable forms rendered differently by context and supported preview, validation, and runtime display rules.
context.coordination: The work required close coordination among web, integration server, application server, and broader flow-platform contributors. Web behavior had to match server logic for temp-board uploads, attachment copying, Variables folder creation, automation execution updates, and resource-path persistence. I worked as the bridge between product scope and engineering implementation for my feature area.
context.challenges: The main challenge was handling file variables as stateful workflow entities rather than simple uploaded files. Files had to survive template creation, start-flow execution, runtime editing, automation configuration, preview, execution, and post-execution reconstruction. Another challenge was preserving variable-to-file mapping after execution when returned attachment payloads no longer contained all pre-execution metadata, which required dedicated restoration logic. The add/edit/submit/load and preview diagrams make this lifecycle visible.
context.outcomes: The final solution enabled file-type variables across workspace creation and runtime, exposed them to DDR/file automation flows, supported Update Workspace Info automation with file-variable awareness, and improved maintainability through utility-based refactoring and clear data modeling. Web and server behavior were aligned well enough for related tasks to move through approved/completed states.

raw_text: Focused contribution across MV-17980, MNF-30, MNF-33, and MNF-548 on workflow file variables and Update Workspace Info automation. Owned T1 technical analysis and interface definition, plus T2 web-side detailed design and implementation. Built file-type workspace variable support, DDR/file source integration, automation file-variable modeling, uploader and preview/runtime behavior, variable order handling, and reusable file-variable utilities. Coordinated web behavior with integration/application server logic for temp-board upload, Variables folder copy/update, and executed-state file rendering.

title: MV-18357 Forms Conditional Logic UX Tune

ownership_classification.background: PM and design defined the business problem and expected UX direction based on customer feedback about poor traceability between form fields/pages and their associated conditional logic.
ownership_classification.t1_contribution: Owned technical scope analysis for the web side: translated approved requirements into interaction flows, component responsibilities, state/interface definitions, implementation approach, and edge-case handling.
ownership_classification.t2_contribution: Contributed to detailed web implementation design and front-end delivery across builder, canvas, and conditional-logic panel components in Vue.
ownership_classification.coordination: Bridged product requirements, UX behavior, engineering implementation details, and QA-sensitive interaction expectations; aligned the feature with existing form-builder architecture and lazy-loading behavior.
ownership_classification.outcome: Improved usability of conditional-logic management by enabling two-way navigation between form elements and logic items, plus search/filter support for faster discovery in large forms.

summary: Worked on a Vue-based form builder enhancement that improved how users manage conditional logic in complex forms. The feature addressed a usability issue where users could no longer easily identify which conditional logic rule was tied to a specific form field or page. My contribution focused on translating approved product and UX requirements into a robust web solution: defining interaction flows, component responsibilities, shared state behavior, and implementation details across the form builder, form canvas, and conditional logic panel. The delivered solution enabled bidirectional navigation between form elements and logic items, supported lazy-load-aware scrolling and highlighting, and added keyword-based filtering with inline match highlighting to improve rule discoverability.
ownership_description: Product direction and UX goals were defined by PM/design. My role was to turn those approved requirements into an implementable and maintainable web solution. I was responsible for technical analysis of the feature scope, defining component/state interaction patterns, handling tricky UI cases such as lazy-loaded elements and synchronized highlighting, and supporting structured Vue-based implementation across the relevant builder modules.
technical_highlights: Defined bidirectional interaction between form elements and the conditional-logic panel so users could navigate from a field/page to its logic rule and from a logic rule back to the controlled field/page.
Designed shared state flow using selectedConditionalLogicId and isLogicTagClicked to distinguish click origin and drive different UI behaviors for selection, scrolling, and highlighting.

Implemented or designed lazy-load-aware navigation logic: when the target form page/field was not rendered, used form metadata to reveal the page first, then re-query, scroll into view, and apply highlight effects.
Separated persistent selection state from temporary highlight state to keep UI feedback clear and predictable.
Added conditional-logic search/filter capability by page/field name, including filtered rendering, empty-state behavior, and inline keyword highlighting within logic labels.
Structured implementation across Vue modules including FormBuilder/FormTemplateBuilder, FormCanvas, FormConditionPanel, and FormConditionItem.
Handled interaction edge cases such as maintaining or clearing search state depending on whether navigation originated from a logic tag or from the logic list.
resume_bullets: Delivered a Vue-based UX enhancement for a form builder’s conditional-logic workflow, enabling bidirectional navigation between form elements and logic rules to improve traceability and editing efficiency.
Translated approved product/UX requirements into detailed web interaction design and implementation across FormBuilder/FormTemplateBuilder, FormCanvas, FormConditionPanel, and FormConditionItem.

Designed state-driven UI coordination using shared element IDs and source-aware selection logic to support synchronized panel focus, form scrolling, persistent selection, and temporary highlight behavior.
Solved lazy-loading navigation issues by revealing hidden form pages from store-backed form metadata before scrolling to and highlighting target fields or pages.
Added searchable filtering for conditional-logic items by field name, including empty-state handling and inline keyword highlighting for faster discovery in large forms.
Supported cross-functional delivery by aligning engineering behavior with product scope, UX expectations, and QA follow-up details.
interview_answer:
One project that reflects my strengths well was a conditional-logic UX enhancement for a web form builder. The business problem was that users could no longer easily tell which logic rule was tied to which form field or page, especially in complex forms. Product and design defined the target UX, and my role was to translate that into a robust web solution.

On the web side, I broke the feature into two linked navigation flows. First, clicking a field or page’s Show/Hide logic tag should open the logic panel, scroll to the corresponding rule, and keep it selected. Second, clicking a conditional-logic item in the panel should scroll to the related form element and briefly highlight it. I also added search and filtering so users could locate logic by field name more quickly.

The technically interesting part was handling lazy loading. The target field or page was not always rendered, so I used form metadata from the builder store to locate the right page, make it visible when needed, then re-query the DOM, scroll to it, and apply the highlight effect. I also separated persistent selection from temporary highlight so the UI remained clear and predictable.

Overall, the project shows how I work: I take approved requirements, turn them into structured component/state behavior, handle the edge cases that appear in real applications, and deliver maintainable front-end solutions in a complex Vue-based system.
self_intro: I work on complex Vue-based web applications, especially workflow-oriented features that require strong technical analysis and structured implementation. In one form-builder project, I translated approved UX requirements for conditional logic into a detailed web solution covering cross-component state flow, lazy-load-aware navigation, synchronized selection/highlighting, and searchable rule filtering. That project reflects both my front-end execution ability and my strength in system-level thinking.
talking_points: The problem came from real customer feedback: users could not easily trace which conditional logic controlled which field or page.
PM/design owned the product direction; I owned the web-side technical analysis and implementation design.
The core solution was two-way navigation between form structure and conditional logic.
A key technical decision was using shared IDs plus a click-source flag to drive different selection and highlight behavior.
One of the hardest parts was lazy loading, because the target page or field was not always rendered in the DOM.
I handled that by using form metadata from the store to reveal the hidden page first, then re-querying, scrolling, and highlighting.
I also separated persistent selected state from temporary highlight state to keep the UI understandable.
Search support improved discoverability by filtering logic items by field name and highlighting matched text inline.
The project is a good example of translating requirements into component contracts, state flow, and maintainable front-end behavior.
It also shows cross-functional collaboration, because interaction details had QA follow-ups and needed clear behavioral definitions.

context.business_background: An earlier UX tuning for form conditional logic made it harder for users to identify which conditional logic item was associated with a specific form field or page. Multiple customers complained that the relationship between form elements and their corresponding logic became difficult to find in larger forms.
context.team_client_requirements: Users needed to quickly move between a form field/page and the conditional logic item that controlled it. They also needed a way to search logic items by field name to improve discoverability when many logic rules existed.
context.pm_decisions: PM/design defined the target UX: clicking a Show/Hide tag on a form page or field should open the conditional logic panel and focus the related rule; clicking a conditional logic item should highlight the corresponding form element; search/filter should be added to the panel and support match highlighting and empty state.
context.t1_responsibilities: Assessed the technical scope on the web side, broke the feature into interaction scenarios, defined the behavior and interfaces between builder, canvas, and panel modules, clarified edge cases such as lazy-loaded targets and search-state handling, and translated approved requirements into engineering-ready design.
context.t2_responsibilities: Contributed to detailed Vue implementation design and front-end behavior across FormBuilder/FormTemplateBuilder, FormCanvas, FormConditionPanel, and FormConditionItem, including prop/event flow, watchers, search filtering, and highlight behavior.
context.architecture_details: At the model/store level, formInfo and related builder state held the page/field metadata required to locate form elements, including cases where targets were not currently rendered. At the controller/component interaction level, form page/field logic tags emitted clickLogicTag events, and conditional logic items emitted clickConditionalLogic events. Builder-level components tracked selectedConditionalLogicId and isLogicTagClicked, then passed those values down to FormConditionPanel and FormCanvas. FormConditionPanel used them to scroll to and select the correct logic item, while FormCanvas used them to reveal hidden pages if needed, scroll to the target form element, apply temporary highlight styling, and remove it after a timeout. Search behavior filtered the conditional logic list and highlighted matching text in item labels.
context.coordination: The work sat between product requirements, UX behavior, web implementation, and QA validation. It required alignment on expected interactions, especially around selection state, highlight timing, search behavior, and edge cases when the target form element was hidden by lazy loading.
context.challenges: The main technical challenge was synchronizing two separate UI regions—the form canvas and the conditional-logic panel—without confusing the user. Another challenge was lazy loading: the target field or page was not always in the DOM, so navigation had to reveal hidden content before scrolling and highlighting. Search behavior also required careful handling, including whether the search key should be retained or cleared depending on navigation source.
context.outcomes: The feature made conditional logic much easier to navigate and manage, especially in large forms. Users could directly trace rules to fields and fields back to rules, and search/filter support reduced the effort required to locate specific logic items. The task was completed and tested, with related follow-up items addressing UX refinements such as placeholder wording, highlight duration, and clearing search state.

raw_text: MV-18357 focused on improving the usability of conditional logic in a web form builder. The business issue came from customer complaints after an earlier UX tuning: users could no longer easily identify which conditional logic item was associated with a particular form field or page. The approved solution introduced two-way navigation between form elements and the conditional-logic panel, plus search/filter support by field name. On the web side, the work involved technical scope analysis, detailed interaction design, Vue component coordination, state propagation, lazy-load-aware element focusing, temporary highlight effects, persistent selection effects, and filtered list rendering with keyword highlighting.

title: MV-18768 Form Enhancement P4 - UX Tuning
category: Web Frontend / Form Builder / UX Tuning

ownership_classification.background: PM-defined scope from PRC-921 follow-up UX tuning task
ownership_classification.t1_contribution: Owned Web technical scope analysis, behavior/interface definition, module impact analysis, and implementation approach
ownership_classification.t2_contribution: Owned or contributed directly to Web detailed implementation design and frontend development across impacted Vue modules
ownership_classification.coordination: Collaborated with design, QA, and broader cross-platform delivery flow; aligned implementation with builder/template workflows
ownership_classification.outcome: Delivered completed Web UX enhancement for image upload flow and show/hide rule clarity

summary: Worked on the Web implementation of a form builder and form template builder UX enhancement focused on two areas: improving image upload behavior for form image and heading elements, and refining the show/hide rule configuration UI. My contribution centered on technical scope analysis, frontend interaction design, interface definition, and structured Vue implementation across shared upload, display, editor, and submission modules. A core technical improvement was enabling immediate thumbnail synchronization in both the canvas and editor while deferring actual resource upload until final submission, which improved editing responsiveness without breaking persistence behavior.
ownership_description: This project was a PM-scoped UX tuning task rather than a product decision I initiated. My role was to translate the approved requirements and design into a workable Web solution. In T1, I was responsible for technical scope analysis, impacted-module assessment, implementation estimation, and behavior/interface definition across the Web builder flow. In T2, I contributed to detailed Web implementation design and development across Vue components handling upload, rendering, editor synchronization, and save-time resource persistence. I also contributed to the Web-side UX tuning for show/hide rule field presentation.
technical_highlights: Defined a temporary-to-persistent image handling flow: cropped images were stored locally as base64/blob-derived cache data plus thumbnail URLs for immediate rendering, then uploaded only during final form or template submission.
Improved reactive synchronization between the form canvas and right-panel element editor so uploaded thumbnails appeared immediately in both surfaces without waiting for backend upload.
Reused and integrated a shared ImageUploadPanel component across FormImage and FormHeading flows to support drag-and-drop, file browsing, preview, and deletion behavior.
Designed field-level image state handling using imageCache, thumbnail URL, image name, and image UUID to separate local editing state from persisted resource state.

Implemented save-time image resource processing in FormBuilder/FormTemplateBuilder by scanning fields, converting cached image data back to blobs, uploading resources, updating URLs/UUIDs, and surfacing upload failure errors.
Handled display logic across editing mode and preview mode by switching between upload panel, placeholder, and resolved image rendering based on field state.
Contributed to UX tuning in conditional show/hide rule configuration to improve page-versus-field hierarchy clarity in selection lists.
Worked across a multi-module workflow-heavy frontend area rather than a single isolated component, reflecting complex application and system-level thinking.
resume_bullets: Delivered Web-side UX enhancements for Form Builder and Form Template Builder, improving image upload interactions and show/hide rule field clarity.
Owned technical scope analysis and frontend design breakdown for a PM-scoped UX tuning task, translating approved requirements into a structured Vue implementation.
Designed a local-cache-plus-deferred-upload image flow using blob/base64 conversion and thumbnail URLs to provide instant visual feedback during editing.
Implemented immediate thumbnail synchronization between form canvas and right-panel editor without waiting for backend resource persistence.
Integrated shared upload behavior across ImageUploadPanel, FormImage, FormHeading, field option components, and builder save flow.
Built save-time image resource handling to upload cached images, update field metadata, and block submission on upload failure.
Contributed to conditional rule UI improvements by refining page and field hierarchy presentation in show/hide dialogs.
Collaborated with design and QA within a structured delivery flow to complete a multi-module workflow builder enhancement.
interview_answer: In this project, I worked on the Web implementation of a form builder UX enhancement. The approved scope had two main parts: improving image upload for form image and heading elements, and refining the show/hide rule configuration UI. My role was not to define the product direction, but to translate the approved PM and design scope into a solid frontend solution. In T1, I analyzed the impacted modules, estimated implementation scope, and defined the interaction and data-handling approach. In T2, I implemented and detailed the Web design across shared upload components, form canvas components, right-panel editor components, and builder submission logic. One of the main technical challenges was that users needed immediate thumbnail feedback after cropping an image, but the actual resource upload should not slow down editing. I solved this by using a temporary local cache approach: storing blob-derived image data and thumbnail URLs in field state for immediate rendering, then converting and uploading the image only at submit time. I also helped improve the show/hide rule configuration UI so page and field hierarchy were clearer. Overall, this project demonstrates my strength in technical analysis, Vue-based structured implementation, interface definition across modules, and workflow-oriented frontend system design.
self_intro: I recently worked on a Web-side enhancement for a form builder system, focused on image upload UX and conditional rule configuration. My contribution covered technical scope analysis, behavior and interface definition, and detailed Vue implementation across multiple modules. A key part of the work was designing a local-cache and deferred-upload flow so image changes felt immediate during editing while still saving correctly at submission time.
talking_points: This was a workflow-builder enhancement, not a simple page-level UI change.
The product scope came from PM and design; my ownership was technical analysis, implementation design, and Web delivery.
A key challenge was separating editing responsiveness from final persistence.
I used local cached image data and thumbnail URLs for instant UI feedback, then uploaded real resources at submit time.
The work required coordination across shared components, canvas rendering, editor rendering, and submission handling.
This project is a good example of structured Vue implementation in a complex stateful application.
It also shows that I can handle interface contracts and data flow design, not just visual coding.
The show/hide rule update reflects my ability to improve usability in workflow-configuration interfaces.

context.business_background: This task was selected as part of the UX tuning scope related to PRC-921 Form Enhancements Phase 4. The goal was to improve form authoring usability in builder flows, especially for image-enabled form elements and rule configuration interfaces.
context.team_client_requirements: Users needed to upload images directly in the form canvas for image and heading elements through drag-and-drop or file browsing, and they needed thumbnails to appear immediately in both the form element and the editor. They also needed clearer page-versus-field hierarchy styling in show/hide rule selection.
context.pm_decisions: PM-defined scope included two core changes: image upload/edit improvements for image and heading form elements, and UX tuning for show/hide rule field selection clarity. Design ownership was assigned separately and should not be presented as my decision.
context.t1_responsibilities: Analyzed Web impact for Form Builder and Form Template Builder, defined behavior and interface expectations, identified affected modules, shaped implementation logic for upload/sync/save flow, and translated approved requirements into a technical breakdown suitable for execution.
context.t2_responsibilities: Contributed to detailed Web implementation design and development for ImageUploadPanel.vue, FormImage.vue, FormHeading.vue, FormFiledImageOption.vue, FormFiledHeadingOption.vue, FormBuilder.vue, FormTemplateBuilder.vue, ShowOrHideFieldDialog.vue, and FormRuleSection.vue.
context.architecture_details: At the data/model level, image state was represented using temporary cached blob/base64 data, thumbnail URL, image name, and image UUID. At the controller/interaction layer, ImageUploadPanel emitted selected file data and deletion events. At the store/state layer, field data was updated reactively with imageCache and image metadata. At the view layer, components switched between upload panel, placeholder, and resolved image rendering depending on preview/edit mode and persisted/local state. Final persistence happened in builder submission logic through saveImageResources, which converted cached data back to blobs and uploaded them.
context.coordination: Worked within a structured delivery flow involving PM-scoped requirements, design handoff, coding, and QA completion. The task also sat in a broader product flow spanning workspace forms, flow templates, and form templates, so implementation needed to remain aligned with shared platform behavior.
context.challenges: The main challenge was enabling immediate image preview synchronization without blocking the editing experience on resource upload timing. Another challenge was keeping behavior consistent across the form canvas, right-panel editor, preview mode, and final submission path. The rule configuration update also required clearer hierarchy presentation in a workflow-heavy UI.
context.outcomes: The Web enhancement was completed. Users could upload or drag files directly into image-enabled form fields, see immediate thumbnail updates in both canvas and editor, and benefit from clearer show/hide rule selection UX. Save-time handling also improved reliability by surfacing upload failures before form/template creation completed.

raw_text: MV-18768 was a Web UX tuning task under the broader PRC-921 Form Enhancements Phase 4 scope. The business goal was to improve usability in form authoring workflows. The approved scope focused on two main areas: first, improving image upload behavior for form image elements and heading elements that support images; second, improving clarity in show/hide rule field selection. Previously, users could not directly drag and drop or browse for files from the form canvas, and thumbnail syncing between the form element and the editor had visible delay after crop confirmation. In the rule UI, page and form field hierarchy was not visually clear enough in the DO section. My role was to translate this approved scope into a Web technical solution. In T1, I handled technical scope analysis, module impact assessment, behavior/interface definition, and implementation thinking for the Web builder flow. In T2, I contributed to detailed Vue implementation design and development across shared upload, display, editor, and builder save modules. A key technical design was to separate editing responsiveness from final persistence by storing cropped image data locally as base64/blob cache plus thumbnail URL, using that state for immediate rendering, and only uploading the final blob resource during form/template submission. This required coordination across ImageUploadPanel.vue, FormImage.vue, FormHeading.vue, field option components, and FormBuilder/FormTemplateBuilder save logic. I also contributed to the Web UX tuning for show/hide rule field list styling and hierarchy clarity. The finished enhancement improved direct image upload, immediate thumbnail synchronization, and rule configuration usability in a complex workflow-oriented form builder environment.

title: MV-18289 Shared Workspaces UX Update
category: Cross-platform profile experience / Web application optimization

ownership_classification.background: PM/business-driven feature update to optimize the Shared Workspaces experience in Contact Profile and reduce unnecessary server load for high-workspace users.
ownership_classification.t1_contribution: Owned technical scope analysis, behavior definition, platform impact assessment, and interface/API alignment across Web, iOS, Android, ISDK, and Biz.
ownership_classification.t2_contribution: Owned web-side detailed implementation design and development planning, including component behavior, state transitions, request flow, cache strategy, retry handling, and lazy-load versus non-lazy-load logic.
ownership_classification.coordination: Coordinated implementation expectations across product, web, mobile, ISDK, Biz, and QA to keep UX behavior, request semantics, and edge cases aligned.
ownership_classification.outcome: Delivered a more efficient and structured shared-workspace experience with deferred loading in standard cases, 6-month filtering, clearer empty/error states, and preserved ACD Binder behavior where preloading remained necessary.

summary: Worked on a cross-platform UX and performance update for the Shared Workspaces section in contact profiles. The PM-defined product change renamed the section to Recently Shared Workspaces, limited results to active shared workspaces from the last 6 months, and avoided loading data by default in most cases. On the web side, I translated those requirements into a structured implementation covering lazy loading, cache validation, in-flight request reuse, retry handling, and a separate ACD Binder path where shared workspaces still had to load on profile open because call/meet eligibility depended on the result.
ownership_description: My responsibility was not to define the product direction, but to turn the PM-owned feature requirements into a technically consistent and implementable solution. In T1, I focused on technical scope analysis, platform and behavior definition, implementation impact, and interface/API alignment across layers and platforms. In T2, I drove the web-side detailed design and implementation structure, including component logic, async request control, state reset rules, caching behavior, error and retry states, and request parameter integration from view to controller to service layers.
technical_highlights: Translated PM-defined UX changes into a web implementation plan with collapsed-by-default behavior, on-demand loading, empty state, error state, and retry interaction.
Designed lazy loading for standard contact and binder profile cases so shared workspaces are only queried when the user expands the section.
Defined cache validation and reuse of ongoing requests to avoid duplicate network calls during repeated expand/collapse actions.
Integrated a filterLatest request mode that maps to a 6-month fromTime/toTime request window in lower layers.
Handled special ACD Binder behavior where lazy loading could not be used because call/meet eligibility depended on shared one-on-one conversation state.
Defined reset behavior for switching user/team and closing profile views so UI state, cache, and pending requests return to a predictable initial state.
Helped preserve existing sorting behavior while applying the 6-month activity filter and removing ACD binders from displayed results where needed.
Worked across view, controller, store/action, ISDK, and Biz-request layers rather than limiting the change to component UI only.
resume_bullets: Led web technical analysis and detailed implementation design for a cross-platform Shared Workspaces UX update in contact profiles, aligning behavior across Web, iOS, Android, ISDK, and Biz.
Translated PM-defined product changes into a structured Vue/web solution with collapsed-by-default loading, 6-month filtering, empty/error/retry states, and clear state reset behavior.
Designed async request control for shared workspace loading, including cache validation, in-flight request reuse, and retry handling to reduce redundant server requests.
Defined interface behavior for filterLatest and mapped it through controller and service layers into timestamp-based backend request parameters.
Handled ACD Binder exception flows where shared workspaces had to load eagerly to support downstream call/meet eligibility logic.
Contributed cross-functionally with product, mobile, server-side, and QA teams to keep UX behavior, request semantics, and edge-case handling consistent.
interview_answer: One project that represents my strengths well is a Shared Workspaces UX update in Moxo. The business issue was that opening a contact profile could trigger an expensive shared-workspace lookup, especially for users with very large workspace counts. The product direction came from PM: rename the section to Recently Shared Workspaces, show only active shared workspaces from the last 6 months, keep the section collapsed by default, and only load data when the user expands it in standard cases. My role was to translate that into a technically consistent cross-platform solution, with particular ownership on the web side. In T1, I handled scope analysis, behavior definition, platform impact assessment, and API/interface alignment across Web, mobile, ISDK, and Biz. In T2, I designed the web implementation in detail: component state transitions, lazy loading, cache validation, reuse of ongoing requests, error and retry handling, and reset behavior when switching users or closing the profile. A key nuance was that ACD Binder cases could not use lazy loading because shared workspaces were needed to determine call and meet eligibility, so I helped define a separate non-lazy-load path. I see this project as a good example of my ability to connect product requirements, frontend state design, backend request semantics, and cross-team coordination in a complex business application.
self_intro: I am a web developer with strong experience in complex business systems where frontend behavior depends on workflow rules, service interfaces, and cross-team coordination. For example, in Moxo's Shared Workspaces UX update, I translated a PM-defined cross-platform feature into a structured web solution covering lazy loading, caching and retry logic, API parameter integration, and multi-layer behavior design across view, controller, store, and service layers.
talking_points: Business problem: loading shared workspaces by default was expensive for users with very large workspace counts.
Product ownership was with PM; my role was technical analysis, behavior definition, interface alignment, and web implementation design.
The feature changed Shared Workspaces into Recently Shared Workspaces and limited results to active shared workspaces from the last 6 months.
Standard contact/binder cases used lazy loading; ACD Binder cases required eager loading because call/meet eligibility depended on shared binders.
Important web design points: collapsed-by-default UI, async loading control, cache validation, promise reuse, retry flow, and predictable reset behavior.
Architecture thinking: the work spanned component/view behavior, controller/store actions, ISDK request semantics, and Biz-layer timestamp parameters.
Cross-platform coordination mattered because behavior had to stay aligned across Web, iOS, Android, ISDK, Biz, and QA.
A useful detail for interviews: sorting logic stayed unchanged; filtering was based on recent activity and cache was cleared on switch or close rather than through live subscription.

context.business_background: Opening a contact profile previously listed Shared Workspaces directly, which required the server to inspect all user workspaces and identify those shared with the selected contact. This became resource-intensive for users with more than 10,000 workspaces.
context.team_client_requirements: The change needed to work across Web, iOS, Android, ISDK, and Biz, and apply to profile under contacts, profile under binder, and profile under ACD binder on web. The experience needed proper empty and failure handling, retry support, and alignment with existing workspace sorting/display behavior.
context.pm_decisions: PM defined the feature direction: rename Shared Workspaces to Recently Shared Workspaces, add a subtitle explaining only active shared workspaces from the last 6 months are shown, collapse the section by default, fetch only when opened in most cases, show empty and failure states, and follow the new P2 UI design.
context.t1_responsibilities: Technical scope analysis, implementation impact assessment, behavior definition, platform coordination, and interface/API alignment across Web, mobile, ISDK, and Biz. This included defining how lazy load behavior, time filtering, retries, and special ACD Binder handling should work across layers.
context.t2_responsibilities: Web-side detailed implementation design and development planning, including SharedBinderPanel and parent integration behavior, watcher logic, state initialization, cache strategy, request deduplication, filterLatest payload handling, and request parameter mapping into lower-layer query calls.
context.architecture_details: The implementation followed a layered structure from view/component to store/action to controller to ISDK/Biz request. On web, SharedBinderPanel handled display and loading behavior; parent profile components passed lazyLoad behavior; controller methods translated filterLatest into a 6-month fromTime/toTime range; final business requests used USER_REQUEST_READ_TIMESTAMP_FROM and USER_REQUEST_READ_TIMESTAMP_TO.
context.coordination: The work required alignment across product, web, mobile, ISDK, Biz, and QA teams so the UX behavior, request semantics, sorting expectations, cache behavior, and special-case handling stayed consistent.
context.challenges: The feature had to reduce unnecessary server load without breaking existing workflow expectations. It also needed to avoid duplicate requests, handle repeated opens/closes cleanly, preserve expected ordering, support retry/error behavior, and accommodate ACD Binder cases where eager loading was still required.
context.outcomes: The result was a more efficient and controlled shared-workspace experience: standard cases only load when the user asks to see the list, results are limited to the most relevant 6-month window, empty/error states are explicit, and special ACD Binder dependency cases remain supported.

raw_text: Cross-platform profile UX/performance update for Shared Workspaces. PM owned the product decision to rename the section, collapse it by default, limit results to the most recent 6 months, and load on demand in standard cases. My contribution was translating that into technical scope, cross-platform behavior definition, API/interface alignment, and detailed web implementation design across component, controller, store, and service layers. Key web contributions included lazy loading, cache validation, request deduplication, retry handling, reset behavior on switch/close, and a dedicated ACD Binder path where eager loading remained necessary.

title: MV-17930 Scheduled Flow Preview Flow Member List Tuning
category: Workflow / Calendar / Member Display / Role-State Handling

ownership_classification.background: The product requirement and scope were driven by a template behavior change introduced in MV-17305, where unassigned roles were no longer discarded when creating or editing flow templates.
ownership_classification.t1_contribution: I was responsible for technical scope analysis on the web side, impact assessment of the template-role change, implementation breakdown, and defining the data/interface behavior needed to distinguish assigned and unassigned roles through the scheduling pipeline.
ownership_classification.t2_contribution: I was responsible for the web-side implementation design and development logic, including request payload handling and member-list rendering behavior in the calendar detail page.
ownership_classification.coordination: I coordinated the client-side contract with server-side storage expectations so the role-state flag could be persisted in workflow variables and consumed consistently by the calendar detail UI.
ownership_classification.outcome: The feature enabled correct assignee/viewer display for scheduled flows by preserving assigned vs. unassigned role state at scheduling/edit time and using that stored state when rendering member lists.

summary: This project improved the scheduled flow preview and calendar detail experience by correcting how flow members were displayed in assignee and viewer lists. After template behavior changed so unassigned roles were no longer discarded, a role's assignee could no longer always be treated as an assignee in the scheduled flow. On the web side, I helped define and implement a stable solution that distinguished assigned and unassigned roles during scheduled flow creation/editing, persisted that distinction in workflow variables, and updated the calendar member-list logic to render assignees and viewers correctly.
ownership_description: PM owned the product direction and scope. My responsibility was the web-side technical analysis, interface and behavior definition, implementation design, and related development work across the scheduling request flow and calendar member display logic. I translated the requirement into a concrete client-side data contract, aligned that contract with server-side storage, and implemented the web logic needed to preserve and display the assigned/unassigned role distinction consistently.
technical_highlights: Analyzed the downstream impact of the template-role behavior change and identified that scheduled flow participant display could no longer rely only on assigneeIds.

Defined a role-state persistence strategy using workflow variable custom_data, specifically custom_data.is_viewer_role, to distinguish assigned and unassigned roles during create/edit flow scheduling.
Used actNumber as the web-side classification signal to determine whether a role was assigned or unassigned when constructing the scheduled flow request payload.
Updated request-payload generation logic to convert IWorkflowRole into WorkflowVar while preserving role-state metadata required by later UI rendering.
Improved calendar detail member computation so assignees and viewers were derived from stored workflow variables plus meeting members rather than fragile runtime inference.
Handled deduplication logic so a user or group appearing in both assigned and unassigned roles would be prioritized in assignees and not duplicated in viewers.
Worked through the feature in a structured way across data/model, controller/request payload, and view-layer member rendering.
Aligned web-client behavior with server-side storage expectations so the persisted role-state flag could be used directly by the calendar detail page.
resume_bullets: Led web-side technical analysis and implementation for MV-17930, a workflow/calendar feature that corrected assignee and viewer display in scheduled flow preview and calendar detail views.

Defined and implemented a role-state persistence approach using workflow variable custom_data.is_viewer_role to distinguish assigned vs. unassigned template roles during scheduled flow create/edit flows.
Used role actNumber to classify assigned and unassigned roles at request-build time, improving consistency between scheduling configuration and downstream UI rendering.
Updated request-payload and Vue rendering logic across scheduling flow modules and calendar member-list computation to correctly display assignees, viewers, and deduplicated participants.
Coordinated client-server interface behavior so stored workflow metadata could be consumed directly by the calendar detail page without extra template resolution at display time.
Improved correctness of scheduled workspace participant display and supported related bug-fix validation for assignee/viewer list behavior.
interview_answer: One project I worked on was MV-17930, which improved how participants were displayed in a scheduled flow preview and calendar detail page. The background was that template behavior changed so unassigned roles were no longer discarded. That created a downstream issue because a role's assignee might actually need to appear as a viewer rather than always as an assignee. My role was on the web side. I analyzed how that change affected the scheduling pipeline, defined how assigned versus unassigned role state should be represented in the request and stored data, and implemented the related web logic. We used actNumber to determine whether a role was assigned or unassigned when building the scheduled flow payload, then stored that state in workflow variables through custom_data.is_viewer_role. On the UI side, I updated the calendar member-list logic so assignees and viewers were derived from persisted workflow variables and meeting members, including deduplication when the same user or group appeared in multiple roles. A key part of the work was cross-layer coordination, because this was not just a UI fix. The web client and server had to agree on how that role-state metadata was stored and consumed. I think this project shows my strength in technical analysis, API and interface thinking, structured Vue implementation, and handling complex system behavior across layers.
self_intro: I have worked on complex Vue-based workflow features where the challenge was not only UI implementation, but also translating product requirements into stable cross-layer behavior. For example, in a scheduled flow preview project, I owned the web-side analysis and implementation for role-state handling, defining how assigned versus unassigned roles should be preserved in workflow data and rendered correctly in calendar participant lists through request-payload and UI logic.
talking_points: This was a system-behavior and data-semantics problem, not just a UI tuning task.
The product scope came from PM and upstream template changes; I owned the web-side technical breakdown and implementation logic.
I translated a business rule into a persisted interface contract using custom_data.is_viewer_role.
I used actNumber to classify role state during request construction instead of relying on display-time inference.
I worked through the stack in a structured way: data model, request/controller flow, and view rendering.
I coordinated client-server behavior so the calendar detail page could rely on stored workflow metadata directly.
I handled edge cases such as duplicate member appearance across assigned and unassigned roles.
I worked within a known constraint that the calendar detail page reflects stored scheduled-flow state rather than later template changes.
This project demonstrates technical analysis, interface definition, Vue implementation, and complex workflow-system thinking.

context.business_background: The scheduled flow preview UI in the calendar originally displayed assignees based directly on the flow's assigneeIds because templates only preserved roles with assigned users and users could not edit schedule flow steps during scheduling/editing. After MV-17305 changed template handling so unassigned roles were no longer discarded, that assumption no longer held.
context.team_client_requirements: The system needed to distinguish assigned and unassigned roles during scheduled flow creation and editing, persist that distinction in the scheduled flow instance, and correctly display assignee and viewer lists in the calendar detail page.
context.pm_decisions: The product decision was to preserve assigned vs. unassigned role state in the scheduled flow instance and use that stored state to drive calendar member-list display, rather than dynamically re-deriving the latest template role usage at display time.
context.t1_responsibilities: I assessed feature impact on the web platform, analyzed how template-role changes affected the scheduling flow pipeline, defined the required request and display behavior, and aligned the web contract with backend storage expectations.
context.t2_responsibilities: I worked on the web-side implementation design and development logic for role-state handling, including request-payload generation in the scheduling flow path and assignee/viewer computation in the calendar member-list UI.
context.architecture_details: At the data/model level, role state was represented in workflow variables through custom_data.is_viewer_role. At the controller/request level, getFlowRolesBody converted IWorkflowRole into WorkflowVar and used actNumber to classify assigned vs. unassigned roles. At the view level, the calendar detail member computation used workflow.variables and meeting members to derive assignees, viewers, and deduplicated final display results.
context.coordination: The feature required web and server agreement on storing and preserving custom_data.is_viewer_role in scheduled flow workflow variables so the calendar detail page could consume persisted role-state metadata directly.
context.challenges: A major challenge was that template behavior had changed upstream, breaking the earlier assumption that a role's assignee always mapped to assignee display. Another constraint was that the calendar detail page reflects stored scheduled-flow configuration and does not automatically reflect later template updates.
context.outcomes: The feature enabled correct participant display for scheduled flows by distinguishing assigned and unassigned roles, placing unassigned-role users or groups into viewers, preserving assignee priority in overlapping cases, and supporting related correctness fixes in the scheduled workspace detail experience.

raw_text: MV-17930 improved scheduled flow preview and calendar detail member-list correctness after flow templates stopped discarding unassigned roles. I owned the web-side technical analysis and implementation logic for preserving assigned vs. unassigned role state through scheduling request payloads and using that persisted metadata to correctly render assignee and viewer lists in the calendar detail page. The solution used actNumber to classify roles, stored the result in workflow variable custom_data.is_viewer_role, aligned the web contract with server-side storage, and updated Vue member-list rendering with deduplication behavior.
